nativeRange.habitat: Wet, shady streamside and forest-floor habitats in West African lowlands; generally grows emergent along stream banks but tolerates fully submerged conditions.

decorPreferences: porous rock (lava, dragon stone) for attachment
vertical space for long lance-shaped leaves

summary: Anubias Afzelii is one of the larger Anubias species, with long lance-shaped leathery leaves carried on creeping rhizomes. It is generally considered an easy, slow-growing epiphyte well suited to background placement attached to driftwood or rock.
careNotes:
Anubias Afzelii (Anubias afzelii) is among the larger Anubias species in the aquarium trade. Its elongated lance-shaped leaves commonly reach 25–40 cm in length, with mature specimens potentially reaching around 60 cm including the petiole (leaf stalk), making it best suited to background placement in larger aquariums. Native to shaded, wet streamside and forest-floor habitats across West Africa — from Senegal through Guinea, Sierra Leone, Mali, Liberia, and Cote d'Ivoire — it thrives in low to moderate light and is not demanding in terms of water chemistry.

Like all Anubias, it is an epiphyte, meaning it grows attached to hard surfaces rather than rooting into soil. The creeping rhizome (the thick horizontal stem from which the leaves grow) should be tied or glued to driftwood or porous rock and kept ABOVE the substrate at all times; burying the rhizome is commonly reported to cause it to rot. Attachment methods include super glue gel, fishing line, or simply wedging the plant into a crevice.

This species is slow-growing and is commonly reported to accumulate algae on its broad leaves under strong lighting; low to medium light is the generally advised default. CO2 supplementation is optional but is reported to improve growth rate and reduce algae pressure. Water temperature of 22–26°C with stable, well-cycled conditions is recommended, as sudden parameter swings have been reported to cause leaf melt.

Liquid fertilization — particularly iron and trace elements — is beneficial, since Anubias draw nutrients primarily from the water column rather than the substrate. Propagation is by rhizome division: cut the rhizome so each section retains at least a few leaves and roots, then attach the divisions to new surfaces. Maximum leaf size typically takes two to three years to develop, so this is a plant that rewards patience. Anubias afzelii is also reported to tolerate emergent growth in paludarium setups, where part of the plant sits above the waterline.

primary.name: Modern Aquarium
primary.notes: Provided care profile: West African origin (Cote d'Ivoire, Sierra Leone, Liberia), elongated leathery green leaves up to ~25 cm tall, temperature 71-78°F (~22-26°C), low light, slow but steady growth (2-3 years to maximum leaf size), midground-to-background placement, rhizome above substrate when planted in gravel, suitability with active/aggressive and herbivorous tankmates, and emerged-or-submerged tolerance.
